response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, applies to any organization worldwide that handles EU residents’ data and mandates rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent, plus 72-hour breach notification and, for large-scale processors, a Data Protection Officer. Non-compliance can cost up to the higher of €20 million or 4 % of global annual revenue, a power used to levy record penalties including €1.2 billion against Meta (2023), €746 million against Amazon, and more than €200 million in total against Google. GDPR has become the template for new privacy laws—from California’s CCPA to Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act—affirming data privacy as a fundamental right. Critics, however, warn that onerous compliance obligations, ubiquitous cookie-consent banners, and uneven enforcement place disproportionate burdens on small firms and may hinder innovation.
